q: Who is responsible for managing the Product Backlog?
exp: The Product Owner is the sole person responsible for managing the Product Backlog.

q: It is a good practice to have at least two Product Owners on big projects.
exp: The Product Owner is one person, not a committee. The PO may represent desires of a committee in the Product Backlog.

q: What is the Sprint Backlog?
opts: The plan for delivering Product Backlog items
The Product Backlog items selected for this Sprint
The Product Backlog items selected for this Sprint plus the plan for delivering them
The Product Backlog items selected for this Sprint plus the Team Backlog items
exp: The Sprint Backlog is the set of Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint plus a plan for delivering the product Increment and realizing the Sprint Goal.

q: The Product Owner should be expertly aware of the marketplace for the product.
exp: The Product Owner should be expertly aware of the marketplace for the product. They should constantly be gathering and re-gathering information.

q: What does Burn-down Chart show?
opts: Hierarchy of tasks that comprise a project
The evolution of the amount of uncertainty during a project
How much work remains till the end of the Sprint
Dependencies, start times and stop times for project tasks
exp: A burn-down chart tracks work remaining vs. time. Scrum does not mandate it but it is a common practice.

q: What happens during the Sprint? (Select three)
opts: No changes are made that would endanger the Sprint Goal
Scope may be clarified and re-negotiated between the Product Owner and Developers as more is learned
Sprint scope is defined at Sprint Planning and cannot be changed
The Sprint Goal is changed frequently to reflect the status
exp: During a Sprint: no changes endangering the Sprint Goal, quality does not decrease, scope may be clarified with the PO.

q: How frequently should product releases occur?
opts: Frequently enough to elicit feedback and allow the team to validate hypotheses about the product
Only when a major feature is complete
exp: Releases should occur frequently enough to gather feedback. Scrum does not mandate releasing every Sprint.

q: What is the Product Goal?
opts: It is the daily objective that needs to be achieved.
It is a long-term objective for the Scrum Team to achieve.
It is the immediate goal that the Developers aims to achieve in the current Sprint.
It is a high-level summary of the project scope.
exp: The Product Goal in Scrum is a long-term objective for the Scrum Team. It provides direction and sets the expectation of what is to be achieved.

q: The purpose of the Sprint Review is...
opts: To review the status of the Project
To check what was done and not done during the Sprint
To inspect the outcome of the Sprint and determine future adaptations
To show Stakeholders work that is done and work that is near to completion
exp: The Sprint Review is held to inspect the outcome of the Sprint and determine future adaptations. The Scrum Team and stakeholders review what was accomplished.

q: Stakeholders may include representatives from Human Resources.
exp: Stakeholders include anyone with an interest in the product — customers, users, management, HR, etc.

q: Who can cancel a Sprint?
exp: Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint, although it is usually a destructive act.

q: What does Product Backlog management include? (Select three)
opts: Ordering the items to best achieve goals and missions
Optimizing the value of the work the Developers performs
Ensuring that the Product Backlog is visible, transparent, and clear to all
exp: Product Backlog management includes expressing items clearly, ordering them, and optimizing the value of Developers' work.

q: The Scrum Master observes the Product Owner struggling with ordering the Product Backlog. What is the most appropriate action?
opts: Offer the Product Owner help in understanding that the goal of ordering the Product Backlog is to maximize value.
Suggest that the Developers order the Product Backlog to ensure a feasible sequence of work.
Encourage the Product Owner to work with the Developers to see which items are easiest to complete.
Tell the Product Owner to prioritize by business value only.
exp: The Scrum Master serves the Product Owner by helping them understand that the goal of ordering the Product Backlog is to maximize value.

q: Who is responsible for the Definition of Done?
exp: The Scrum Team is responsible for creating the Definition of Done. If it is not an organizational standard, the Scrum Team creates it.

q: True or False: The Sprint Goal can be updated during the Sprint.
exp: The Sprint Goal is set during Sprint Planning and does not change during the Sprint. If work threatens the Sprint Goal, the Developers negotiate scope with the Product Owner.

q: How long is the Sprint Retrospective for a one-month Sprint?
exp: The Sprint Retrospective is time-boxed to 3 hours for a one-month Sprint. For shorter Sprints, the event is usually shorter.

q: What is the maximum length of a Sprint?
exp: Sprints are fixed length events of one month or less. When a Sprint is too long the Sprint Goal may become invalid.

q: The Increment must be in a useable condition at the end of every Sprint.
exp: The Increment must be in a useable condition regardless of whether the Product Owner decides to release it.

q: Who is responsible for maximizing the value of the product?
exp: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product resulting from the work of the Scrum Team.

q: What are the Scrum Values? (Select five)
exp: The five Scrum Values are: Commitment, Courage, Focus, Openness, and Respect.

q: True or False: The Product Owner can delegate Product Backlog management to the Developers.
exp: The Product Owner may do the above work or may delegate the responsibility to others. However, the Product Owner remains accountable.

q: When does the Sprint end?
opts: When all Sprint Backlog items are complete
When the Product Owner approves the Increment
When the Sprint Goal is met
exp: The Sprint ends when the time-box expires. It is not extended and work is not moved to the next Sprint automatically.

q: Which artifact provides transparency on the progress toward the Product Goal?
exp: The Product Backlog is an emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product. The commitment for the Product Backlog is the Product Goal.

q: The commitment for the Sprint Backlog is the...
exp: The Sprint Goal is the commitment for the Sprint Backlog. It provides flexibility while keeping the team focused.

q: Who creates the Sprint Goal?
opts: The entire Scrum Team during Sprint Planning
exp: The Sprint Goal is created by the entire Scrum Team during Sprint Planning. The Product Owner proposes how the product could increase its value.

q: How many people are in the Scrum Team?
exp: The Scrum Team is small enough to remain nimble and large enough to complete significant work, typically 10 or fewer people.

q: The Developers are structured and empowered to...
opts: Organize and manage their own work
Report to the Product Owner daily
exp: The Developers are structured and empowered to organize and manage their own work. This increases efficiency and accountability.

q: Product Backlog Refinement is...
opts: The act of breaking down and further defining Product Backlog items
The responsibility of the Scrum Master
exp: Product Backlog refinement is the act of breaking down and further defining PBI to smaller more precise items. It is an ongoing activity.

q: What is the time-box for the Daily Scrum?
exp: The Daily Scrum is a 15-minute event for the Developers. It is held every working day of the Sprint.

q: Who attends the Daily Scrum?
opts: The Developers and the Scrum Master
The Developers and the Product Owner
exp: The Daily Scrum is an event for the Developers. The Scrum Master ensures it happens but Developers are responsible for conducting it.

q: What is the purpose of the Sprint Retrospective?
opts: To review the product Increment with stakeholders
To plan ways to increase quality and effectiveness
To identify items for the Product Backlog
exp: The Sprint Retrospective is to plan ways to increase quality and effectiveness. The Scrum Team inspects how the last Sprint went.

q: True or False: Multiple Scrum Teams working on the same product should have separate Product Backlogs.
exp: Multiple Scrum Teams working on the same product must use the same Product Backlog. There is one Product Owner and one Product Backlog.

q: The three pillars of empiricism in Scrum are...
exp: Scrum is founded on empiricism. The three pillars are Transparency, Inspection, and Adaptation.

q: How long is the Sprint Planning event for a one-month Sprint?
exp: Sprint Planning is time-boxed to 8 hours for a one-month Sprint. For shorter Sprints, the event is usually shorter.

q: The Product Owner is accountable for the Product Backlog including its content, availability, and ordering.
exp: The Product Owner is accountable for the Product Backlog — its content, availability, and ordering — to maximize the value of the product.

q: What does the Scrum Master do during Sprint Planning?
opts: Ensures the event takes place and everyone understands its purpose
exp: The Scrum Master ensures that the Sprint Planning event takes place and that attendants understand its purpose.

q: Which of the following best describes Scrum?
opts: A lightweight framework for developing and delivering complex products
A project management approach for simple projects
A set of practices for software engineering
exp: Scrum is a lightweight framework that helps people, teams and organizations generate value through adaptive solutions for complex problems.

q: The Product Owner must be available during Sprint Planning.
exp: Sprint Planning involves the entire Scrum Team. The Product Owner ensures that the Product Backlog items selected are clear and that the Sprint Goal is set.

q: What is the definition of 'Done'?
opts: All Sprint Backlog items are completed
A formal description of the state of the Increment when it meets the quality measures required for the product
All code is deployed to production
exp: The Definition of Done is a formal description of the state of the Increment when it meets quality measures required for the product.

q: An Increment is...
opts: The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during a Sprint plus prior Sprints
Only the new features developed in the current Sprint
The work completed by the Developers
exp: Each Increment is additive to all prior Increments and thoroughly verified, ensuring all Increments work together.

q: The Product Owner should have one set of priorities for the Product Backlog.
exp: The Product Backlog has one ordering/priority managed by one Product Owner. Competing priorities should be reconciled by the PO.

q: True or False: Scrum requires a specific engineering practice.
exp: Scrum does not prescribe specific engineering practices. Teams choose their own techniques and practices within the Scrum framework.

q: What is the role of the Scrum Master regarding the Product Owner?
opts: To manage the Product Owner's schedule
To help the Product Owner find techniques for effective Product Goal definition and Product Backlog management
To order the Product Backlog on behalf of the Product Owner
exp: The Scrum Master serves the Product Owner by helping find techniques for effective Product Goal definition and Product Backlog management.

q: Who participates in the Sprint Review?
opts: The Scrum Team and key stakeholders
Only the Product Owner and Developers
exp: The Sprint Review is attended by the Scrum Team and key stakeholders invited by the Product Owner.

q: Can the Sprint Goal change during the Sprint?
opts: Yes, if the Product Owner requests it
No, the Sprint Goal is fixed once set
Yes, if the Developers request it
exp: The Sprint Goal does not change during the Sprint. It gives the Developers flexibility regarding the exact work needed to achieve it.

q: Scrum is defined as a complete, rigid process that must be implemented exactly as described.
exp: Scrum is intentionally incomplete and only defines the parts required to implement Scrum theory. It is a framework, not a rigid process.

q: What happens to incomplete Sprint Backlog items at the end of a Sprint?
opts: They are automatically moved to the next Sprint
They are returned to the Product Backlog
exp: Incomplete Product Backlog items are returned to the Product Backlog and re-estimated. The PO decides whether to include them in the next Sprint.

q: Who owns the Sprint Backlog?
exp: The Sprint Backlog is a plan by and for the Developers. It is a highly visible, real-time picture of the work they plan to accomplish.

q: The Product Backlog is never complete.
exp: The Product Backlog is dynamic; it constantly changes to identify what the product needs to be appropriate, competitive, and useful.

q: How does the Product Owner maximize the value of the Developers' work?
opts: By ordering the Product Backlog and communicating clearly what needs to be built and why
exp: The Product Owner maximizes value by managing the Product Backlog — ordering items to achieve the highest value from the Developers' work.

q: Scrum Teams deliver products iteratively and incrementally, maximizing opportunities for feedback.
exp: Incremental deliveries ensure a potentially useful version of working product is always available. This maximizes opportunities for feedback.

q: The Scrum Master is a servant-leader for the Scrum Team.
exp: The Scrum Master serves the Scrum Team, the Product Owner, and the organization. The Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the Scrum Team.

q: Who is responsible for creating the Increment?
exp: The Developers are the people in the Scrum Team committed to creating any aspect of a usable Increment each Sprint.

q: What are the accountabilities in Scrum?
opts: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team
exp: The Scrum Team consists of one Scrum Master, one Product Owner, and Developers. Within a Scrum Team, there are no sub-teams or hierarchies.

q: True or False: The Developers can choose how to structure and plan the work in the Sprint.
exp: No one tells the Developers how to turn Product Backlog items into Increments of value. They self-manage to accomplish this.

q: What does the Product Owner use to order Product Backlog items?
opts: Business value, risk, dependencies, and other factors
exp: The Product Owner orders items considering value, risk, dependencies, and any other factor that helps achieve goals and missions.

q: True or False: The Scrum Master enforces the rules of Scrum.
exp: The Scrum Master is accountable for the Scrum Team's effectiveness. They help everyone understand Scrum theory and practice.

q: What is the purpose of the Product Backlog?
opts: To document all requirements for the project
To provide an emergent ordered list of what is needed to improve the product
exp: The Product Backlog is an emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product. It is the single source of work for the Scrum Team.

q: True or False: The Scrum Master can be the same person as the Product Owner.
exp: Combining the Scrum Master and Product Owner roles is not recommended as these accountabilities have very different focuses.

q: The Sprint Review is a time to demonstrate the Increment and collect stakeholder feedback.
exp: During the Sprint Review, the Scrum Team presents the results of their work and progress toward the Product Goal is discussed.

q: What is velocity in Scrum?
opts: The speed at which Developers write code
A measure of the amount of work a team can complete per Sprint
exp: Velocity is a measure of the amount of work completed per Sprint. Scrum does not mandate velocity, but teams often track it for planning.

q: True or False: The Product Owner must be a member of the Scrum Team.
exp: The Product Owner is a member of the Scrum Team. The Scrum Team consists of one Product Owner, one Scrum Master, and Developers.

q: What is the key concern when multiple Scrum Teams work on the same product?
opts: Each team should have its own Product Backlog
They should all work from the same Product Backlog
Each team should have its own Definition of Done
exp: Multiple Scrum Teams working together on the same product use a single Product Backlog with one Product Owner.

q: What is Scrum's approach to technical debt?
opts: Technical debt should be avoided through the Definition of Done
Technical debt is tracked in a separate backlog
Technical debt is the responsibility of the Scrum Master
Technical debt is managed outside Scrum
exp: The Definition of Done creates transparency by ensuring the Increment meets quality standards, which helps prevent technical debt.

q: Who can add items to the Sprint Backlog during the Sprint?
exp: The Developers can modify the Sprint Backlog throughout the Sprint as they learn more. No one else makes changes to it.

q: True or False: Scrum prescribes using user stories for Product Backlog items.
exp: Scrum does not prescribe how Product Backlog items are expressed. User stories are a common technique but are not part of Scrum.

q: What is the Scrum Master's role regarding stakeholders?
opts: To help stakeholders understand Scrum and how to interact with the Scrum Team
To prevent stakeholders from talking to the Developers
To report project status to stakeholders
exp: The Scrum Master serves the organization by helping employees and stakeholders understand and enact an empirical approach.

q: True or False: The Sprint Retrospective occurs before the Sprint Review.
exp: The Sprint Review occurs before the Sprint Retrospective. Order: Sprint Planning → Daily Scrums → Sprint Review → Sprint Retrospective.

q: The Sprint Retrospective is an opportunity for the Scrum Team to...
opts: Inspect itself and create a plan for improvements
exp: The Sprint Retrospective is for the Scrum Team to inspect how the last Sprint went and identify improvements.

q: A Product Owner can represent the needs of multiple stakeholders in the Product Backlog.
exp: The Product Owner may represent the desires of a committee in the Product Backlog but must have a single set of priorities.

q: When should Product Backlog Refinement occur?
opts: As an ongoing activity throughout the Sprint
Once per Sprint in a dedicated meeting
Only when the Product Backlog is empty
exp: Product Backlog refinement is an ongoing activity. Teams often dedicate up to 10% of Sprint capacity to refinement.

q: What must be true of every Increment?
opts: It must be released to end users
It must be approved by the Product Owner
It must meet the Definition of Done
It must contain at least one new feature
exp: An Increment must meet the Definition of Done. Only then is it considered part of the Increment — regardless of whether it is released.

q: Who decides the order of items in the Product Backlog?
opts: The Developers based on technical complexity
exp: Ordering the Product Backlog is the sole responsibility of the Product Owner, who optimizes value based on various factors.

q: True or False: Scrum is free and offered in the Scrum Guide.
exp: The Scrum Guide is a free resource. Scrum itself is a framework available for anyone to use as described in the Scrum Guide.

q: What does Empiricism in Scrum mean?
opts: Knowledge comes from planning and prediction
Knowledge comes from experience and decisions based on what is observed
Knowledge comes from following a prescriptive process
exp: Scrum is founded on empiricism and lean thinking. Knowledge comes from experience and decisions are made based on what is observed.

q: True or False: The Product Owner can tell the Developers how to technically implement Product Backlog items.
exp: The Developers decide how to implement items. No one tells the Developers how to turn PBI into Increments — they self-manage.

q: What is included in Sprint Planning? (Select two)
opts: What can be Done this Sprint?
How will the Developers report progress to management?
exp: Sprint Planning addresses: Why is this Sprint valuable (Sprint Goal), What can be Done (PBI selection), and How will chosen work get done.

q: True or False: The Product Owner approves each Increment before it is considered Done.
exp: The Definition of Done determines when an Increment meets quality measures. The Product Owner does not personally approve each Increment.

q: A Scrum Team consists of...
opts: A Project Manager, Developers, and Testers
A Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Developers
A Product Owner, Project Manager, and Developers
A Scrum Master, Team Lead, and Developers
exp: The Scrum Team consists of one Scrum Master, one Product Owner, and Developers. There are no sub-teams or hierarchies.

q: True or False: Sprint length can vary from Sprint to Sprint.
exp: Sprint length should be consistent. Shorter Sprints can be employed to generate more learning cycles and limit risk.

q: What is the best description of a Product Owner's interaction with stakeholders?
opts: The Product Owner avoids stakeholders to focus on the backlog
The Product Owner manages and collaborates with stakeholders to understand and prioritize their needs
The Product Owner delegates all stakeholder communication to the Scrum Master
The Product Owner only meets with stakeholders during Sprint Reviews
exp: The Product Owner collaborates with stakeholders, represents their interests in the Product Backlog, and invites them to Sprint Reviews.

q: True or False: The Daily Scrum is a status meeting for the Scrum Master.
exp: The Daily Scrum is not a status meeting — it is a planning event for the Developers. The Scrum Master ensures it happens but does not run it.

q: Who is responsible for enacting Scrum?
exp: The entire Scrum Team is responsible for enacting Scrum. Within the team, different accountabilities are assigned to each role.

q: What is the time-box for the Sprint Review for a one-month Sprint?
exp: The Sprint Review is time-boxed to 4 hours for a one-month Sprint. For shorter Sprints, the event is usually shorter.

q: The Product Backlog items that can be Done by the Scrum Team in a Sprint are said to be...
exp: Product Backlog items that can be Done by the Developers within one Sprint are deemed 'Ready' for selection in Sprint Planning.

q: True or False: Scrum has a defined process for handling bugs.
exp: Scrum does not prescribe specific practices for handling bugs. Teams decide how to manage bugs within the Scrum framework.

q: What does the Product Owner do during the Sprint?
opts: Inspects progress at the Daily Scrum
Collaborates with stakeholders and Developers, answers questions about PBI, and may update the Product Backlog
exp: The PO clarifies Product Backlog items, collaborates with stakeholders, and may adjust the Product Backlog during the Sprint.

q: A new stakeholder requests a feature midway through a Sprint. What should the Product Owner do?
opts: Add it to the Sprint Backlog immediately
Evaluate it, add it to the Product Backlog if valuable, and prioritize accordingly for a future Sprint
Ask the Scrum Master to decide
exp: New requests go into the Product Backlog. The PO evaluates their value and orders them accordingly — the Sprint is not disrupted.

q: True or False: A Scrum Team should not change its composition during active Sprints.
exp: Changing team composition during a Sprint disrupts cohesion and reduces predictability. Teams should remain stable across Sprints.

q: Scrum is based on which type of process control theory?
exp: Scrum is based on empirical process control theory. Knowledge comes from experience and decisions are based on what is observed.

q: True or False: The Increment must be released at the end of every Sprint.
exp: The Increment must be in a useable condition but the Product Owner decides whether to release it. Multiple Increments may be created in a Sprint.

q: What is the Product Vision?
opts: A detailed list of all product requirements
A clear, inspiring description of what the product will achieve in the long term
exp: The Product Vision describes the long-term goal and purpose of the product. It guides the Scrum Team and stakeholders.

q: How should the Scrum Team handle impediments?
opts: The Product Owner resolves all impediments
Developers resolve impediments themselves; the Scrum Master helps with organizational impediments
Impediments are documented and reviewed at Sprint end
exp: Developers resolve their own impediments where possible. The Scrum Master removes impediments beyond the team's control.

q: True or False: The Scrum Guide recommends specific tools for managing the Product Backlog.
exp: The Scrum Guide does not prescribe specific tools. Teams choose their own tools to manage the Product Backlog.

q: What is the relationship between the Sprint Goal and the Sprint Backlog items?
opts: Sprint Backlog items are selected to achieve the Sprint Goal
The Sprint Goal is derived from Sprint Backlog items
They are created by different people
exp: Sprint Backlog items are selected to achieve the Sprint Goal. The work may evolve, but the Sprint Goal should remain stable.

q: Who should the Product Owner work with to define acceptance criteria for Product Backlog items?
opts: Stakeholders, Developers, and other relevant parties
exp: The Product Owner works with stakeholders, Developers, and others to clarify PBI details, including acceptance criteria.

q: True or False: The Scrum Team should have all the skills necessary to create value every Sprint.
exp: Scrum Teams are cross-functional, meaning the members have all the skills necessary to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team.

q: What is the relationship between Scrum and agility?
opts: Adopting Scrum makes an organization automatically agile
Scrum is a framework that helps teams become more agile through iterative, empirical work
Agility is a prerequisite for using Scrum
exp: Scrum is a framework based on empiricism and lean thinking. Using Scrum helps teams become more agile by generating feedback and adapting.

q: True or False: Scrum Teams should not interact with customers until the Sprint Review.
exp: Scrum Teams should interact with customers and stakeholders frequently. The Sprint Review is one opportunity but not the only one.

q: Who forecasts how much work can be completed in a Sprint?
exp: The Developers forecast how much work they can complete in a Sprint during Sprint Planning. No one else decides this.

q: True or False: The product roadmap is a required Scrum artifact.
exp: The Scrum Guide defines three artifacts: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, and Increment. A product roadmap is not a required Scrum artifact.

q: What is the key measure of progress in Scrum?
exp: The primary measure of progress in Scrum is working, Done product. This provides real feedback on the product's development.

q: True or False: The Product Owner can accept or reject work results at the end of a Sprint.
exp: The Product Owner may accept or decline the work results. Work meeting the Definition of Done is accepted as part of the Increment.

q: All of the following types of requirements are acceptable on a Product Backlog: stability requirements, performance requirements, product functionality, documentation, fixes.
opts: Yes, they all belong on the Product Backlog. It is the Single Source of Truth for all work for the product.
No, only user-facing features belong on the Product Backlog.
Only requirements approved by management belong on the Product Backlog.
Only functional requirements belong on the Product Backlog.
exp: The Product Backlog is the single source of work for the Scrum Team. Any type of work needed to improve the product can be included.

q: Who is responsible for monitoring progress toward high-level goals?
opts: The Product Owner and The Development Team
The Scrum Master and The Development Team
exp: The Product Owner tracks total work remaining at least every Sprint Review.

q: What are the Scrum Artifacts? Select all applicable items.
exp: The Scrum artifacts are Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog and Increment.

q: What could be a source of requirements for any changes to be made to the product?
exp: The Product Backlog is the single source of requirements for any changes to be made to the product.

q: Who is responsible for the Product Backlog?
opts: The Product Owner and The Scrum Master
The Product Owner and The Development Team
The Scrum Master and The Development Team
exp: The Product Owner is responsible for the Product Backlog, including its content, availability, and ordering.

q: What are Product Backlog features? Select three.
opts: As long as a product exists, its Product Backlog also exists
When the final version of a product is rolled out, its Product Backlog is dismissed
A Product Backlog could be closed when it contains no items to include into the next Sprint
exp: A Product Backlog is never complete. It is dynamic — it constantly changes to identify what the product needs to be appropriate, competitive, and useful.

q: All Development Teams working on the same Product should use the same Product Backlog.
exp: Multiple Scrum Teams often work together on the same product. One Product Backlog is used to describe the upcoming work on the product.

q: Who is responsible for all estimates in the Product Backlog?
opts: The Product owner and the Development Team
The Scrum Master and the Development Team
The Product owner and the Scrum Master
exp: The Development Team is responsible for all estimates in the Product Backlog. The Product Owner may influence the Developers by helping them understand trade-offs.

q: Who is responsible for tracking the total work remaining in the Sprint Backlog to project the likelihood of achieving the Sprint Goal?
opts: The Product Owner and the Development Team
exp: The Development Team tracks total work remaining in the Sprint Backlog at every Daily Scrum to project likelihood of achieving the Sprint Goal.

q: Who is allowed to change the Sprint Backlog during the Sprint?
opts: The Development Team and the Product Owner
exp: Only the Development Team can change its Sprint Backlog during a Sprint. It is a real-time picture of the work that the Development Team plans to accomplish.

q: What is the Increment?
opts: The sum of all the Product Backlog items completed during the Sprint
The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during the Sprint and the value of increments of all previous Sprints
All Done items in the Sprint Backlog
All items in the Sprint Backlog that could be released
exp: The Increment is the sum of all PBIs completed during the Sprint and the value of the increments of all previous Sprints.

q: Who is responsible for creation of the Definition of Done?
exp: If DoD is not an organizational standard, the Development Team creates it. If multiple teams share a product, they must define DoD together.

q: Who is allowed to participate in the Daily Scrum?
exp: The Daily Scrum is an internal meeting for the Development Team. If others are present, the Scrum Master ensures they do not disrupt the meeting.

q: What is the order of items in the Product Backlog?
opts: Less valuable and most unclear items at the bottom
The recently added items at the top
The less clear items at the top
exp: The Product Owner places the most valuable and clear items at the top. Less valuable and unclear items are at the bottom.

q: All the Scrum Teams working on the same product should have the same Sprint length.
exp: Scrum does not require aligned Sprint lengths for multiple teams working on the same product.

q: How does the Scrum Master help the Product Owner? Select three.
opts: Facilitating Scrum events as requested or needed
Finding techniques for effective Product Backlog management
Understanding product planning in an empirical environment
Leading and coaching the organization in its Scrum adoption
exp: The Scrum Master serves the PO by facilitating Scrum events, finding techniques for PB management, and helping understand product planning empirically.

q: What does Cone of Uncertainty show?
opts: Hierarchy of tasks that comprise a project
How much work remains till the end of the Sprint
How much is known about the Product over time
Dependencies, start times and stop times for project tasks
exp: The Cone of Uncertainty describes the evolution of the amount of uncertainty during a project. Early on, much is unknown; it narrows over time.

q: If a Sprint Backlog item cannot be finished by end of Sprint, the Sprint is cancelled.
exp: The Sprint is cancelled only if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete. Unfinished items return to the Product Backlog for re-negotiation.

q: How does the Definition of Done help the Scrum Team? Select three.
opts: DoD is used to assess when work is complete on the product Increment
Guides the Development Team in knowing how many PBIs it can select during Sprint Planning
DoD helps in inspection and adaptation
exp: DoD assesses increment completeness, guides sprint planning capacity, and ensures artifact transparency.

q: What part of the Development Team capacity does Product Backlog refinement usually consume?
opts: The Development Team is not authorized for Product Backlog refinement
exp: Product Backlog refinement usually consumes no more than 10% of the capacity of the Development Team.

q: In which Scrum events can people outside the Scrum Team participate?
exp: Outsiders may participate in Sprint Planning (by invitation) and Sprint Review. Sprint Retrospective is for the Scrum Team only. Daily Scrum is for Developers.

q: What are the three most applicable characteristics of the Product Owner?
opts: Lead Facilitator of Key Stakeholder Involvement
Lead Scrum evangelist in the Organization
exp: The PO is: Product Value Maximizer, Lead Facilitator of Key Stakeholder Involvement, and Product Marketplace Expert.

q: The Sprint Backlog is created at Sprint Planning. New work cannot be added to it by the Development Team later.
exp: The Development Team modifies the Sprint Backlog throughout the Sprint as more is learned. The Sprint Backlog emerges during the Sprint.

q: In which meetings are Key Stakeholders allowed to participate?
exp: Key Stakeholders are allowed to participate only in the Sprint Review. Any Scrum Team member can interact with them any time, however.

q: Who is allowed to make changes in the Product Backlog? Select two.
opts: The Development Team, but with permission of the Product Owner
exp: The PO is solely responsible for the Product Backlog but may delegate some management to the Development Team with permission.

q: Who is responsible for crafting the Sprint Goal at Sprint Planning?
exp: After the Development Team forecasts the PBIs it will deliver, the Scrum Team crafts a Sprint Goal together.

q: Who participates in Sprint Planning? Select three.
exp: Sprint Planning is planned by the collaborative work of the entire Scrum Team: Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team.

q: The Scrum Team consists of...
exp: The Scrum Team consists of a Product Owner, the Development Team, and a Scrum Master. There are no sub-teams or hierarchies.

q: Who is allowed to tell the Development Team to work from a specific set of requirements?
opts: The Product Owner and the Scrum Master
exp: Only the Product Owner, through the ordered Product Backlog, tells the Development Team what to work on. No one else gives them work orders.

q: The Development Team should be able to explain to the Product Owner and Scrum Master how it intends to work as a self-organizing team to accomplish the Sprint Goal.
exp: By end of Sprint Planning, the Development Team should be able to explain how it intends to work as a self-organizing team to accomplish the Sprint Goal.

q: Product Backlog Refinement... Select three most applicable endings.
opts: Is the act of adding detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items
Usually happens 2-4 times depending on Sprint length
Answers how work will be achieved
Usually takes no more than 10% of Development Team capacity
exp: Product Backlog refinement is the act of adding detail, estimates, and order to PBIs. It is ongoing and consumes no more than 10% of team capacity.

q: Which KVA categories should the Product Owner consider to measure value delivery? Select three.
exp: Evidence Based Management focuses on: Current Value, Time-to-Market, and Ability to Innovate as key value area categories.

q: Who is the chief product visionary?
exp: The Product Owner is the chief product visionary, articulating the product vision to the Scrum Team and key stakeholders.

q: Who should do the legwork of gathering marketplace data for the Product Owner?
opts: It does not matter who does the legwork
The Scrum Team and the Key Stakeholders
exp: The PO may or may not do the legwork personally, but must be aware of the data and results to inform Product Backlog decisions.

q: How does the Product Owner communicate marketplace knowledge to the Scrum Team? Select three.
exp: The PO communicates market knowledge through daily interactions, Product Backlog Refinement, and Sprint Reviews.

q: Who decides whether to release the latest Increment of the product?
opts: The Product Owner and The Scrum Master
exp: The Product Owner is the one and only person who can decide whether to release the latest Increment of the product.

q: What factors should the Product Owner consider in the release decision? Select four.
opts: The risk that product value gets out of line with the marketplace
Can customers absorb the new release?
The costs and benefits of the upgrade
The customers constrained by the new release
Amount of work remaining toward Sprint Goal
Does the Increment meet the Definition of Done?
exp: Release decisions consider: marketplace risk, customer absorption capacity, upgrade cost/benefit, and constrained customers.

q: Who identifies the Key Stakeholders for the Product?
exp: The Product Owner identifies Key Stakeholders to maximize value. The PO must understand their needs and represent them in the Product Backlog.

q: If multiple Stakeholders have varied interests and viewpoints, what is the best strategy for the Product Owner?
opts: Do an intelligent balancing of interests and try to maximize the value of the Product as a whole
Listen to the people that fund the product because they always have the last word
Stick to the viewpoint promising the fastest time-to-market
Calculate ROI for every viewpoint and select the maximal one
exp: The PO weighs and balances differing stakeholder viewpoints to maximize overall product value — not just the loudest or richest voice.

q: Where can Scrum be used? Check all applicable items.
opts: Research and identifying of viable markets, technologies, and product capabilities
Development and sustaining of Cloud and other operational environments
Managing the operation of an organization
Development of almost everything we use in our daily lives
exp: Scrum has been used to develop software, hardware, embedded software, networks, autonomous vehicles, schools, government, and more.

q: What is the essence of Scrum?
opts: A small team of people that is highly flexible and adaptive
The Scrum Master and the Product Owner
exp: The essence of Scrum is a small team of people. The individual team is highly flexible and adaptive.

q: Who is responsible for promoting and supporting Scrum?
opts: The Scrum Master and the Product Owner
exp: The Scrum Master is responsible for promoting and supporting Scrum as defined in the Scrum Guide, helping everyone understand its theory and practice.

q: At Sprint Retrospective, the team identified improvements. What should they do?
opts: Include at least one high priority process improvement in the next Sprint Backlog
Include all improvements in the next Sprint Backlog
Assign responsible members for every improvement and check progress at next Retro
Assign one member for at least one improvement and check at next Retro
exp: To ensure continuous improvement, the Sprint Backlog for the next Sprint should include at least one high priority process improvement identified at the Retrospective.

q: Who has the last say on the order of items in the Product Backlog?
opts: The Product Owner and The Scrum Master
exp: The Product Owner is the sole person responsible for managing the Product Backlog, including its ordering. No one can override the PO.

q: Product Backlog Refinement focuses on items for upcoming Sprints, not the current Sprint in progress.
exp: Product Backlog refinement focuses on upcoming Sprint items. Items in the current Sprint are part of the Sprint Backlog and managed by Developers.

q: What are characteristics of a Product Backlog Item Ready for Sprint Planning? Select three.
opts: Can be Done within one Sprint
Somewhere at the top of the Product Backlog
Somewhere at the bottom of the Product Backlog
Can be implemented in one Sprint and tested in the next
exp: Ready PBIs can be Done in one Sprint, are near the top of the backlog, and are well-refined with enough detail for planning.

q: Technical debt... Select three best options.
opts: Is a real risk which can genuinely be incurred
Compromises long-term quality of the Product
Belongs entirely to the Development Team — no one else should know
Is a lack of technical supplies
exp: Technical debt is a real risk that compromises long-term product quality and reflects extra development work needed due to shortcut decisions.

q: The Scrum Master should not allow the Product Owner to attend Sprint Planning if the PO is not ready with a Sprint Goal.
exp: False. The Scrum Team crafts a Sprint Goal together during Sprint Planning. The PO does not need a pre-defined Sprint Goal to attend.

q: When something about Scrum frustrates the Product Owner, the best action is to...
opts: Raise concerns at the Sprint Retrospective
Ask the Scrum Master to handle it
Ignore it and focus on the Product Backlog
exp: The Sprint Retrospective is the correct forum for the Scrum Team to inspect itself and plan improvements, including addressing frustrations.
